ordinance: Santa Monica — 4.08.095
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Santa Monica had an existing, unenforceable camping ordinance. In September 2024, they amended it to reinstate and enforce it. 4.08.095 Prohibition against camping in public places. Ordinance extends even to sleeping bags, pillows, and blankets.

ordinance: Baldwin Park — codes 99.01-99.04
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Existing codes 99.01-99.04 banned camping, which was unenforceable until Grants Pass decision. Enforcement has likely begun again, as shown in a July 2024 internal staff memo recommending it.

ordinance: Hermosa Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: 10/31/24 Press release said that anti-camping ordinances will be enforced post Grants Pass. Language: "Enforcing Public Camping & Loitering Regulations: In line with the recent Supreme Court Grants Pass decision, local laws prohibit camping or sleeping in vehicles on public streets and overnight camping on the beach. Hermosa Beach also enforces laws that prevent loitering in public spaces that obstruct pedestrian movement or cause quality of life impacts."  - Hermosa is currently considering a new ordinance - return!

ordinance: Arcadia — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: City of Arcadia website - Arcadia’s anti-camping laws paused enforcement after *Martin v. Boise*, and have resumed post-Grants Pass

name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Existing ordinance 9.28.010-9.28.040 prohibited camping. Glendale city council voted to resume enforcement.

ordinance: Long Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Long Beach PD now has discretion to enforce existing anti-camping ordinance. Internal memo primarily discusses citations, but also arrests, especially following repeated complaints or "related criminal conduct." Long Beach has been conducting widespread sweeps.

ordinance: Malibu — 8.36.050
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Per Malibu city website - said that Grants Pass won't change much, but did specify 72-hour notices to vacate encampments on public property, when they violate 8.36.050. Will cite unhoused people with "cite and release" system.

ordinance: Irwindale — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: 2024 election - Irwindale passed a measure for a slight sales tax increase to fund, among many other things, "cleaning up homeless encampments."

ordinance: Arcadia — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Arcadia had an existing camping ordinance that was unenforceable under Martin. Per their website, they will resume enforcement.

ordinance: San Dimas — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: San Dimas actually amended their municipal code (not just changing enforcement for an additional code, post Grants Pass). That's code 1310. It is standard language, authorizing citations for storage of belongings on public property and erecting campsites/using camp equpment, with general language about promoting the health and safety of the community.

ordinance: Pasadena — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Probably enforcing their standard 2016 camping ordinance. Although the most recent news (September 2024) only said that the mayor and City Council were "discussing" actions in light of Grants Pass, and that they were not criminalizing homelessness, they did receive a $2.7 million "Encampment Resolution" grant from the state in October.

ordinance: San Fernando — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Actually adopted a new ordinance - "Prohibit Encampments and Storage of Personal Property in Public Places" - prosecution as a misdemeanor or public nuisance. 72 hour notice of sweeps.

ordinance: Signal Hill — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Signal Hill updated its camping ordinance in August 2024 to enforce anti-camping post-Grants Pass. Extends to camping, "camp facilities," and "camp paraphernalia.

ordinance: Torrance — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Torrance updated its camping ordinance to prohibit camping in public places, fires in parks and public places, "maintaining shelters," and "loitering at bus stops."

ordinance: Whittier — Ordinance no. 3105
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Whittier had an existing camping ordinance and released a statement of support post-Grants Pass, though hasn't updated its municipal code. Is probably enforcing the existng ordinance, which extends to bedding. Ordinance no. 3105.

ordinance: Redondo Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Redondo Beach, in accordance with Grants Pass, may restrict camping near supportive housing.

ordinance: Manhattan Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Manhattan Beach's camping provision (4.140), enacted shortly before Boise, and includes pillows and sleeping materials, and personal property, is now being enforced.

ordinance: Los Angeles — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: The City of LA has been resisting Newsom's encampment clearance policies, however already had an anti-camping ordinance on the books, pre-Boise. Reporting on that ordinance says that it cost $3 million and permanently housed two people.

ordinance: El Segundo — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: 9/17/24 - El Segundo City Council amended the municipal code with new camping ordinances. Encampments on public property will be given 24 hour clearance notices. PATCHES team offers outreach.

ordinance: Carlsbad — (no code section cited in source)
name: Aug. 2024
text:
Aug. 2024
City of Carlsbad accepted $5.3 mil from Encampment Resolution Funding Program to "address" State Route 78 encampments.

ordinance: Carlsbad — (no code section cited in source)
name: Aug. 2024
text:
Aug. 2024
City of Carlsbad accepted $5.3 mil from Encampment Resolution Funding Program to "address" State Route 78 encampments.

ordinance: Chula Visa — Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.020 (2024)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
"'Campsite' means any place where
(1) bedding, sleeping bag, or other material used for bedding purposes,
(2) or any stove or fire is placed, established, or maintained for the purpose of maintaining a temporary or permanent place to live, whether or not such place incorporates the use of any tent, lean-to, shack or any other structure."
 Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.020 (2024)

ordinance: Chula Visa — Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.030 (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits Sitting, Lying, Sleeping, or Storage on a street, sidewalk, or public property "in a manner that impedes passage, as provided by the ADA."
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.030 (2024).

ordinance: Chula Visa — Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.040 (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Cannot camp
(1) w/in 1000 ft of a school,
(2) in or w/in 1000 ft of a city park,
(3) wildlife conservation areas,
(4) "Major Transit Stops," lists 3 trolley stops and 225 bus stops,
(5) w/in 1000 ft of a shelter/safe camping/safe parking site.
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.040 (2024)

ordinance: Chula Visa — (no code section cited in source)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
(1) police may remove personal property; (2) camping is a public nuisance; (3) violation may be charged as misdemeanor or infraction.

ordinance: Chula Visa — 9.90.030
name: Notice Requirements
text:
Notice Requirements
(1) At least 3 hrs written notice before violating 9.90.030; At least 24 hrs written notice before removal of property
(2) If City Manager/Police Department finds it "reasonably necessary to remove [a] immediate threat," or if SDC directs city to remove property for public health and safety, may remove property w/out any notice.
Chula Vista, Cal., Code § 9.90.050 (2024).

ordinance: Coronado — Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.010 (2015)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping/sleeping in public parks/beaches 11:00pm-5:00am.
(2) No campling/sleeping on any public street
(3) No loitering on public streets 11:00pm-5:00am.
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.010 (2015).

ordinance: Coronado — Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.012 (2015)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) No tents/canopies/barricades in public parks/beaches 11pm-5am
(2) Can't leave tents/canopies/barricades unattended 5am-11pm
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.012 (2015).

ordinance: Coronado — Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.040
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Can't stand/sit on street/median/sidewalk in a manner that obstructs passage or to "hinder/molest/annoy" passerbys
Coronado, Cal., Code § 40.48.040.

ordinance: Del Mar — Del Mar, Cal., Code § 9.04.020
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping overnight.
(2) Camping evidenced by bedrolls and tents
Del Mar, Cal., Code § 9.04.020.

ordinance: El Cajon — El Cajon, Cal. Code § 2.56.010
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
(1) Camping in parks = living/spending inordinary amount of time in a park with "camp paraphenelia"
(2) Camp paraphenelia "includ[es], but [is] not limited to, clothing, sleeping bags, bedrolls, blankets, sheets, luggage, backpacks, kitchen utensils, cookware, and similar material"
El Cajon, Cal. Code § 2.56.010.

ordinance: El Cajon — El Cajon, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.020, 9.36.025
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits sleeping or camping on city property
El Cajon, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.020, 9.36.025.

ordinance: El Cajon — El Cajon, Cal. Code § 12.08.010-20
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) Cannot occupy public street/sidewalk/alley for any purpose besides normal pedestrian traffic.
(2) May not loiter in a manner that "maliciously obstruct[s] or interfere[s] with free passage."
El Cajon, Cal. Code § 12.08.010-20.

ordinance: Encinitas — (no code section cited in source)
name: Introduced Feb. 2025
text:
Introduced Feb. 2025
Proposes collaboration between CA DOT and local gov'ts to clear existing homeless encampments on DOT property and prevent new encampments from forming.
S.B. 569

ordinance: Encinitas — Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) No camping on public property
Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004).

ordinance: Encinitas — Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
"'Camping' is evidenced by, among other things, the establishment of sleeping accommodations; the use of a bedroll or sleeping bag; the erection or maintenance of a shelter"
Encinitas, Cal. Code § 9.01.020 (2004).

ordinance: Escondido — Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-7
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
Camping = "remaining for prolonged or repetitious periods of time not associated with ordinary recreational use of a park with one's personal property (including, but not limited to, clothing, sleeping bags, bedrolls, blankets, sheets, tents, tarpaulins, luggage, backpacks, kitchen utensils, cookware, and similar material), sleeping or making preparations to sleep, regularly cooking or consuming meals, or living in a parked vehicle"
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-7

ordinance: Escondido — Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.4 (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
No camping
(1) where there's an "unreasonable risk of harm" to people/public health/public safety or a disruption of vital gov't services
(2) w/in 500 ft of a school zone
(3) w/in 500 ft of a shelter if there's visible signage
(4) In any "open space, waterway, or bank of a waterway."
(5) in transit hubs(7) any park or open space where city manager considered camping to pose a substantial public health and safety
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.4 (2024).

ordinance: Escondido — Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.5 (2024)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
(1) Violation is a misdemeanor offense
(2) Subject to removal after 24 hr. written notice/expedited abatement
Escondido, Cal. Code § 17-8.5 (2024).

ordinance: Imperial beach — Imperial Beach, Cal. Code § 12.5
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) Prohibits camping, lodging, or sleeping on or w/in any public property.
(2) Prohibits storage of personal items on public proeprty
Imperial Beach, Cal. Code § 12.5.

ordinance: La Mesa — (no code section cited in source)
name: Jan. 2025
text:
Jan. 2025
La Mesa Councilmember calls for policy of removing a homeless person from the city if help is turned down 15 times. No bill.

ordinance: La Mesa — La Mesa, Cal. Code §§ 10.52.020, 10.52.030
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
(1) Camping is not limited to tents and can be anywhere where bedding is placed
(2) No person shall camp in or upon any city sidewalk, street, alley, lane, park, public right-of-way or any other place to which the general public has access, or under any bridgeway or viaduct
La Mesa, Cal. Code §§ 10.52.020, 10.52.030

ordinance: Lemon Grove — Lemon Grove, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.090
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Prohibits camping or storage of property in any public place or street
Lemon Grove, Cal. Code §§ 9.36.090.

ordinance: Lemon Grove — Lemon Grove, Cal. Code § 9.36.100
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
Violation = misdemeanor + fine < $500 or prison < 6 mo.
Lemon Grove, Cal. Code § 9.36.100.

ordinance: National City — National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.020 (2024)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.020 (2024).

ordinance: National City — National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.040 (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code § 10.55.040 (2024).

ordinance: National City — National City, Cal. Code §§ 10.55.050, 10.55.060 (2024)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
Has adopted San Diego City's code.
National City, Cal. Code §§ 10.55.050, 10.55.060 (2024).

ordinance: Oceanside — Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(b) (2024)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
(1) Camping = "to pitch or occupy camp facilities, or to use camp paraphernalia, for living purposes in an outdoor area." NOT sleeping alone
(2) Camp facilities = tents/huts/shacks/other temp.shelters
(3) Camp paraphernalia = but is not limited to, tents or tent-like structures, cots, beds, hammocks or personal cooking facilities and similar equipment. NOT blankets/sleeping bags/bedrolls
(4) Obstruct ped./veh. traffic.= "take evasive action to avoid physical contact"
(5) Open space = unimproved and designated for open space use
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(b) (2024).

ordinance: Oceanside — Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(c) (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) Cannot camp/occupy/use camp paraphenelia in a public space
(2) Cannot sit "on . . . any object" in a manner that obstructs ped./veh. traffic
(3) Cannot sleep in open space
(4) Cannot cook food in public space
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.1(c) (2024).

ordinance: Oceanside — Oceanside, Cal. Code § 202.21.2(b) (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Cannot store personal property (including bed rolls) on public property unattended
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 202.21.2(b) (2024)

ordinance: Oceanside — Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.2(d) (2024)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
(1) Property may be removed after 24 hr written notice
(2) Property may be reclaimed w/in 90 days by identifying property and approx. location it was left
Oceanside, Cal. Code § 20.21.2(d) (2024).

ordinance: Poway — Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.020 (2023)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
Camping is
(1) Residing in or using any public area or private property for living accommodation or habitation purposes
(2) constructing/maintaining/using camping facilities or paraphenelia even if not in active use (bedding included)
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.020 (2023)

ordinance: Poway — Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.030 (2023)
name: Prohibited Activities
text:
Prohibited Activities
Cannot camp on public property or private property w/ permission
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.030 (2023).

ordinance: Poway — Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.070 (2023)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
(1) 24 hrs. written notice before removal
(2) Property may be reclaimed w/in 90 days (unclear on what instructions will provided to owners)
Poway, Cal. Code § 8.26.070 (2023).

ordinance: San Diego — San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0402 (2024)
name: Definitions
text:
Definitions
(1) "Camp" or "Camping" means to place, pitch, erect, or occupy an encampment, or to use camp paraphernalia or both, for the purpose of, or in a way that facilitates outdoor sheltering for living, accommodation or habitation purposes or for remaining outdoors overnight.
(2) "Camp Paraphernalia" includes, but is not limited to, tarps, cots, beds, sleeping bags, hammocks, bedrolls, camp stoves, cooking equipment, buckets, and similar equipment or items that are accessory to encampments, mattresses, couches, dressers, or other
San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0402 (2024).

ordinance: San Diego — San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0404 (2024)
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
Public nuisance to camp, use camp paraphenelia or accumulate waste
(1) on public property
(2) w/in 2 blocks of a school
(3) in waterways
(4) w/in transit hubs
San Diego, Cal. Code § 63.0404 (2024).

ordinance: San Diego — San Diego, Cal. Code §§ 63.0405-06 (2024)
name: Enforcement
text:
Enforcement
(1) Encampments may be removed
(2) Violation is a misdemeanor
San Diego, Cal. Code §§ 63.0405-06 (2024).

ordinance: San Marcos — San Marcos, Cal. Code § 10.33 (2024)
name: Has adopted San Diego City's code
text:
Has adopted San Diego City's code
San Marcos, Cal. Code § 10.33 (2024)

ordinance: Santee — Santee, Cal. Code § 7.20.020 (2019)
name: Defintions
text:
Defintions
Camping includes use of bedding
Santee, Cal. Code § 7.20.020 (2019).

ordinance: Santee — Santee, Cal. Code §§ 7.20.030-60
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) sitting or lying down on the sidewalk or any object placed on the sidewalk in most city zones
(2) loitering where no loitering signs have been posted and verban notice has been given
(3) loitering around public transportation facilities after 48 hr. verbal notice
(4) camping w/in 1000 ft of a waterbody
(5) camping in a park
(6) camping on sidewalks or streets in a manner that obstructs traffic
Santee, Cal. Code §§ 7.20.030-60

ordinance: Solana Beach — Solana Beach, Cal. Code §§ 6.24.010-20
name: Prohibited Conduct
text:
Prohibited Conduct
(1) Unlawful to pitch tent/create temp. structure in public space
(2) May not sleep in public space within any camp/shelter
Solana Beach, Cal. Code §§ 6.24.010-20 (

ordinance: Newport Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Passed Aug. 2024 further restricting camping
text: Passed Aug. 2024 further restricting camping

ordinance: Cypress — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: 3/11/2024 (pre-GP) there was a hearing to adjust their park rules, no overnight camping in parks

ordinance: Huntington Beach — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Seems like the anti-homelessness laws have been strict and unchanged since 2022

ordinance: Irvine — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Nov 2024 Irvine has no homeless shelter, but ability clear out homeless shelters with only 24 hours notice,

ordinance: Banning — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Unlawful camping ordinance. Specifies that sleeping on its own does not constitute camping, but includes sleeping bags and bedrolls as "camp paraphernalia" such that if sleeping bags/bedrolls are used "in such a way as will facilitate sleeping," it is unlawful camping. Adopted 4/23/2024 (pre-Grants Pass)

ordinance: Banning — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Sitting, lying, sleeping in public places is unlawful if obstructing traffic, between 6am and 10pm. Includes sleeping in vehicle parked in public. From 10pm to 6am, sleeping prohibited in public UNLESS no sleeping/shelter spaces practically available. Adopted 4/23/2024 (pre-Grants Pass)

ordinance: Beaumont — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Unlawful camping/unlawful storage of personal property ordinance. Encapsulates public/private property. Includes bedrolls as "camp paraphernalia." Adopted 9/3/2024 (post-Grants Pass)

ordinance: Canyon Lake — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Sitting, lying, sleeping, or storing property in public right of way prohibited. Adopted 10/6/2021.

ordinance: Canyon Lake — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Camping prohibited on public/private property, including sleeping in a vehicle. Adopted 7/10/2013.

ordinance: Calimesa — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Two proposed ordinances related to homelessness in November 2024 city council meeting agenda. Agenda explicitly references Grants Pass decision as motivation to prepare ordinances "specifically related to homelessness." One prohibits unlawful camping/using camp paraphernalia on public property, obstructing right of ways, sitting and lying on public benches, & public urination/defecation; the other declares shopping cart use for personal belongings a public nuisance

ordinance: Cathedral City — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits sleeping vehicles parked in public places. Adopted in 1982.

ordinance: Coachella — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags. Adopted 2/10/2016.

name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Camping and storage of personal property prohibited on public property. (`78 Code, § 9.26.020.) (Ord. 3160 § 4, 2013; Ord. 2833 § 3 (part), 2006; Ord. 2619 § 1, 2002: Ord. 2256 § 1, 1995.)

ordinance: Desert Hot Springs — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Unlawful to camp or use camp paraphernalia (including sleeping bags) on any street, public parking lot, or private property, and to store personal property in any park, street, public property, or private property without owner/tenant consent. Adopted 12/3/2019.

ordinance: Eastvale — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 9/5/2024 (post-Grants Pass)

ordinance: Hemet — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Under "disorderly conduct," prohibits obstructing public property by sitting/lying/storing personal property (adopted 1/11/2022), eating/resting as well as sleeping in a vehicle (adopted 7/8/2003), and camping on private property (adopted 1/11/2022)

ordinance: Indian Wells — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2012.

ordinance: Indo — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping and storing personal property in public spaces. Camping explicitly includes sleeping "for living accommodation purposes" and occurs whether or not a full night is spent. Amended 8/21/2024 (post-Grants Pass). Staff report discussing amendment references Grants Pass decision as motivation for amendment.

ordinance: Jurupa Valley — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 9/5/2024 (post-Grants Pass). Exception for "emergency public health and safety activities."

ordinance: La Quinta — § 8, 2018
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Camping/remaining overnight prohibited in public parks and recreation areas. One of many provisions under "prohibited conduct" in parks/rec section. No specific language appearing to target homeless population. (Ord. 568 § 8, 2018; Ord. 510 § 1, 2013; Ord. 360 § 1, 2001; Ord. 10 § 1, 1982)

ordinance: Lake Elsinore — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: No comprehensive unlawful camping ordinance, but three separate ordinances that are relevant. One prohibits camping on private property without owner's consent (adopted 2015), one prohibits camping on public parks and beaches (adopted 2016), and one declares shopping carts a public nuisance (adopted 2016).

name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Sets out procedure to refer homeless people sitting/lying/sleeping on public property when no shelter is available to "Outreach & Engagement Personnel" before arresting or citing them. BUT states that "These procedures under § 11.21.030(B)(2) are only mandatory during the timeframe that the case of Martin v. City of Boise 902 F.3d 1031 (9th Cir. 2018) is applicable law within the jurisdiction of the Ninth Circuit." Adopted 10/20/2021.

ordinance: Moreno Valley — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property on public streets, sidewalks, right-of-ways, parks, and any other improved or unimproved public land. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2004.

ordinance: Norco — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: "Camp space and squatter camps" ordinance prohibitng camping on public/private property unless a number of sanitation requirements are met. Camping defined differently than in many other codes: "'Camp space' is any place where one or more tents are erected or maintained for hire for living or sleeping quarters for one or more families, or where one or more tents or camp cars or trailers or where one or more families are camping with the expressed consent of the owner or person legally in charge of the land upon which such camp space is located." Adopted 1964.

ordinance: Palm Desert — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping/using camp paraphernalia and storing personal property in public. "Camp paraphernalia" includes sleeping bags and bed rolls. Adopted 2009.

ordinance: Palm Springs — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Explicitly bans encampments & lays out specific procedures for clearing them. Explicitly bans encampments regardless of shelter bed availability in a number of situations, including "unreasonable risk to harm or public safety," when there is a "Disruption to any business, store, religious institution, educational facility, or government services," and "in any open space, waterway, or banks of a waterway." Probably the most comprehensive and strict anti-homeless law I've encountered.

ordinance: Perris — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision

Terms not defined. Adopted 1970. Under use of park and recreation facilities: "No person shall tent, camp, lodge, or otherwise be present in any park or recreation facility after 11:00 p.m. or before 6:00 a.m." Adopted 1992.

ordinance: Santa Clara — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
"Good Neighbor Program": The ordinance would prohibit camping within Water Resources Protection Zones, which include all lands where Valley Water owns property or has easements. Violations could result in fines of up to $500 and jail time of up to 30 days.

The ordinance would ban:
1. Unauthorized establishment of encampments
2. Any unauthorized presence in established zones after sunset
3. Depositing trash, debris, or hazardous pollutants related to encampments
4. Keeping animals in established zones
5. Activities that disturb neighboring properties or create hazards for Valley Water employees or the public

ordinance: San José — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
The city of San José has adopted new restrictions on encampments near schools:

1. The city council unanimously voted to ban all homeless encampments within 150 feet of K-12 schools citywide by establishing "School Clearance Zones".
2. This rule formalizes and slightly expands on a similar policy the city already had in place
3. Violators would be given a $0 administrative citation, with no criminal enforcement

ordinance: San José — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
San José has also implemented tougher rules on RV parking:

1. The city can now tow large vehicles in areas where parked cars create safety hazards for drivers, pedestrians, and cyclists.
2. This policy is particularly focused on areas near schools

ordinance: Albany — Section 8-4.4
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Section 8-4.4 of the municipal code addresses camping specifically:

1. It states that “it is unlawful for any person to engage in camping in any City-owned property”
2. Where camping means “to set up, pitch, occupy, or leave camp facilities or use camp paraphernalia” (§8-4.2)
3. HOWEVER, it “will not be enforced against indigent homeless persons sitting, lying, or sleeping on City-owned public property” with some exceptions
4. This section of the municipal code appears to have been last amended on June 7, 2021

ordinance: Berkeley — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
On Sept. 10, 2024, City Council approved legislation that broadened the city’s authority to sweep camps even when shelter isn’t available.

1. The resolution said that the city wouldn’t sweep encampments unless it was able to offer shelter to displaced people, but it also outlined several exceptions including encampments that posed a health or safety hazard.
2. Also on Sept. 10, 2024, nine business and property owners sued the city over a longstanding homeless encampment, citing public and private nuisances.
3. On Jan. 7, 2025, the city posted notice that officials had found such nuisance conditions at a longstanding encampment and directed everyone living there leave by Feb. 10 or the city would remove all property remaining on the property.
4. On Feb. 13, the Berkeley Homeless Union sued the city to enjoin Berkeley to sweep the encampment.
5. As of Feb. 14, a federal judge has temporarily blocked Berkeley from clearing the encampment.

ordinance: Dublin — Section 5.100.110
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Section 5.100.110 identifies pohibited acts/activities in parks. It states that "unless expressly authorized by the Director, no person while in any park shall: . . . E. Camp, lodge or remain overnight . . ."

ordinance: Fremont — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: In February 2025, Fremont City Council gave final approval to an ordinance that bans camping throughout the entire city, while also making anyone "aiding, abetting or concelaing" a homeless encampment guilty of a misdemeanor. On March 4, they voted to remove the aiding and abetting clause after pushback from the community.

ordinance: Hayward — Section 4-17.20)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Article 17 of Hayward's municipal code addresses camping and storage of personal property:

1. It’s unlawful to camp, occupy camp facilities or use camp paraphernalia on any City-owned property. (Section 4-17.20)
2. It’s unlawful to store personal property, including camp facilities and camp paraphernalia on any City-owned property. (Section 4-17.25)
3. A violation is declared a public nuisance which may be enjoined by civil action or enforced with citations. (Section 4-17.30)
4. “Camp” means to pitch or occupy camp facilities; to use camp paraphernalia. (Section 4-17.10)
5. Appears to have been last updated in 2014.

ordinance: Livermore — Chapter 12.65
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapter 12.65 of the municipal code addresses camping. In livermore it's unlawful and a public nuisance to camp, establish/maintain/operate/occupy camping facilities, or use camp paraphernalia on any public property or any private proeprty where the public has right of access. It's simiarly unlawful to store camp paraphernalia on public property. aA violation is punishable by a fine up to $100 for a first offense, and as a misdemeanor punishable by a fine up to $300 or  up to 30 days in jail or both.

ordinance: Newark — Section 7.04.030
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Section 7.04.030 talks about how unoccupied swellings are attractive for "unlawful camping and occuptation" but doesn't seem to clearly discuss unlawfulcamping elsewhere in the municipal code.

ordinance: Oakland (county seat) — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: On Sept. 24, 2024, Oakland’s mayor gave an executive order to city departments to fully remove people living in homeless encampments, enforcing the Encampment Management Policy. The EMP was passed in 2020, prohibiting encampment within a certain number of feet of schools, parks, playgrounds, bodies of water and retail businesses. It also prohibits most of the cooking and heating equiment used by peope living in encampemnts.

ordinance: Piedmont — Chapter 18
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Piedmont doesn't appear to have any ordinances specifically addressing camping or homelessness, aside from generally prohibiting obstructions of streets and sidewalks (Chapter 18, Article IV, Section 18.22)

ordinance: Pleasanton — Section 13.08.120
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
(A) No person shall camp or lodge in a tent or on the ground in any park and recreation favility, including the parking lot area of any such facility.
(B) Except during daylight, or except for security purposes and with the written permission of the director, no person shall stay, remain or sleep in a motor home or other motor vehicle otherwise, in any park and recreation facility, including the parking lot area of such facility.
(Section 13.08.120 Camping–Sleeping, Ord. 1428 Section 4, 1989)

ordinance: San Leandro — Section 3-10-925)

text: It is unlawful to camp overnight or to park a trailer coach overnight upon any public street or other public place. (Section 3-10-925)

ordinance: Sacramento — Chapter 9.120
name: Proposed anti-camping provision

ordinance: Rancho Cordova — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: The Rancho Cordova City Council unanimously approved a new camping ordinance on Sept. 16, 2024 aimed at updating the city’s approach to managing illegal camping in public spaces.

ordinance: Folsom — Chapter 9.100
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: The Folsom Police Department started its homeless outreach team (HOT), which will ban unlawful camping on public property, authorities said. Planning to enforce the unlawful camping ordinance, outlined in the Folsom Municipal Code (Chapter 9.100 CAMPING).

ordinance: Elk Grove — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
Ordinance No. 12-2024: an ordinance of the city council of the city of Elk Grove amending chapter 9.38 of the Elk Grove Municipal Code related to unlawful camping. Elk Grove City Council unanimously passed an amendment to a city ordinance Wednesday that shortens the window of notice city officials must give to those at homeless encampments, or unlawfully camping in the city, to 24 hours before temporarily seizing their property. The previous notice was a 72-hour window. The amendment, the city said, will allow them to respond quicker to camp clean-ups.

Elk Grove will also prohibit the following: “sleeping on public sidewalks, streets, or alleyways; sleeping in any pedestrian or vehicular entrance to public or private property abutting a public sidewalk; occupying a campsite upon any sidewalk, street, alley, lane, public right of way, park, bench, or any other public property and/or under any bridge; and camping on private property without the consent of the property owner.”

ordinance: Antioch — Antioch, Cal., Mun. Code § 10-2.12 (1996)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Antioch has a strict anti-camping provision on the books, and the mayor announced plans to strengthen it. Antioch, Cal., Mun. Code § 10-2.12 (1996). As far as I can tell, the ordinance hasn't been changed, but it might be enforced more strictly now.

ordinance: Fresno — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Fresno County (in its entirety) will be one of the first local jurisdictions in the country to implement anti-encampment ordinances.

1. On Aug. 20, 2024 the Fresno County Board of Supervisors passed an anti-encampment ordinance, with enforcement set to begin within the next month.
2. Ordinance violators expect to face criminal penalties including fines and jail time.

ordinance: Clovis — Section 5.27.510
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
1. Sleeping and camping in public and certain private places is prohibited.
2. Camping includes use of tents/temporary shelters, use of sleeping bags/beds/hammocks, use of park benches/similar structures, and use of any parked vehicles.
3. It’s not a violation to sleep in a public park between 7am-10pm, or from 10pm-7am in a designated overnight camp area.
4. It is not considered recreational camping when a camp is established with the intent to lodge or reside at that location, remain in that location or park overnight, or remain for a period of more than one consecutive day.
5. In addition to any other remedy or enforcement mechanism, any camp in violation of this section is declared to be a public nuisance, and the Chief of Police or his/her designee is authorized to remove the camp after giving the occupant the opportunity to remove the camp.
(Section 5.27.510 Camping)

ordinance: Sanger — Section 42-15
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
1. No person shall camp, lodge, sleep or tarry overnight in any park or recreation area without written permission from the director.
2. No person shall set up tents, shacks, or any other temporary shelter for the purpose of camping in any park or recreational area without written permission of the director.
3. No person shall leave in a park or recreation area any moveable structure or special vehicle to be used or that could be used for camping, such as a house trailer, camp trailer, motor home, or the like, without written permission from the director.
(Section 42-15 Overnight Camping)

ordinance: Reedley — Section 7-3-5
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: It shall be unlawful for any person to camp within any park or recreation area without having first secured a written permit from the City and paid the applicable fee therefor to the City, and no person shall camp outside of a camp site or site designated in such permit. (Section 7-3-5 Camping; Ord. 2018-004, 8-28-2018)

ordinance: Selma — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: In Oct. 2024, Selma City Council approved an ordinance that prohibits sitting, lying, sleeping or camping in a public place, including but not limited to sidewalks, street alleys, sensitive areas or within doorways. It went into effect in November but is not reflected in the online municipal code.

ordinance: Parlier — Section 6.11.510
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Sleeping and camping in public and certain private places prohibited
“Camp” means an outdoor place for eating, cooking or sleeping.
It is not considered a violation to sleep in any public park between 7am-10pm.
(Section 6.11.510 Camping)
Note: this ordinance seems very similar to Clovis'

(a)No person shall camp, lodge, sleep or tarry overnight in any park or recreation area.(b)No person shall set up tents, shacks, or any other temporary shelter for the purpose of camping in any park or recreational area.(c)No person shall leave in a park or recreation area any moveable structure or special vehicle to be used or that could be used for camping, such as a house trailer, camp trailer, motor home, or the like

It is unlawful for any person to camp or live on any lot or area of land within the city other than in a permanently constructed building with toilet and other facilities required by this code. This provision is intended to prevent persons from sleeping or camping, whether in tents or otherwise, in or upon open areas, such as yards and lots, whether public or private, in the city.

ordinance: Firebaugh — Section 9-1.2
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: It shall be unlawful for any person to inhabit any trailer coach standing or parked in or upon any lot or area of land within the City of Firebaugh, or upon any street, alleyway, or place therein and it shall be unlawful for any person to inhabit any tent, hut, or motor home, or to camp on the ground within any such areas in the City of Firebaugh except in a legally constituted and licensed trailer park or camp, provided that when the city may permit it for specified occasions, camping in city parks may be allowed. name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision

(Ord. 220 § 1, 1986)

1. Sleeping and camping in public and certain private places is prohibited.

3. It’s not a violation to sleep in a public park between 7am-10pm, or from 10pm-7am in a designated overnight camp area.
4. It is not considered recreational camping when a camp is established with the intent to lodge or reside at that location, remain in that location or park overnight, or remain for a period of more than one consecutive day.

ordinance: Fresno — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Fresno's law, passed Aug. 15, 2024, is among the toughest in the state: banning camping, sitting or lying on public property anytime, anywhere.

name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Prohibits camping and using camp paraphernalia, which includes even "materials intended to be used as beds or bedding." Storing personal property in public spaces is also prohibited, even if moved between public spaces throughout the day. This ordinance defines everything broadly, apparently to target activity of homeless population. Exception: "Absent exigent circumstances posing an immediate threat to the public health, safety, or welfare, the provisions of 8.330.030(A) will not be enforced against individuals based solely upon the fact that they are sitting, lying, or sleeping on city-owned public property, when such individuals do not have access to adequate shelter space." (aligns with Boise, ripe for amendment in light of Grants Pass). Adopted 2/8/2022.

ordinance: Bakersfield — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Camping prohibited on public property. Sleeping outside on public property prohibited between sunset and sunrise. Adopted 1988.

ordinance: Bakersfield — Municipal Code 12.56.050
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Amendment to Municipal Code 12.56.050 (prohibited acts in parks) currently pending codification. Amendment replaces the following language: "Be in or upon any park between the hours of ten p.m. and five a.m., except for participants in city-sponsored events, for which the prohibited hours shall be twelve midnight to five a.m.; provided, however, that camping and lodging overnight may be permitted when such camping or lodging is part of an authorized recreational outing under the supervision of the division of recreation and parks of the city pursuant to a permit..." with "Be in or upon any park between the hours of ten p.m and five a.m....No person shall construct or maintain any building, structure, tent or any other thing in any park that may be used for housing accommodation or camping." Passed 11/6/2024 (post-Grants pass). The last time this provision was updated was 2007. Meeting agenda doesn't contain any clues as to why this change was made other than removing "extraneous permit language."

ordinance: San Francisco — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision

He also made permanent the Drug Market Agency Coordination Center, a centralized hub for local, state and federal law enforcement agencies to disrupt drug dealing and public drug use in the Tenderloin and SOMA neighborhoods.

ordinance: San Francisco — 2.12.25
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
2.12.25 Ordinance - Mayor Lurie has authority to bypass traditional approvals to enact his agenda on homelessness. This ordinance temporarily eases procurement and approval rules to speed up the City’s response to homelessness, drug crises, mental health needs, and public safety hiring. Key provisions include:
- Procurement Waivers: Competitive bidding rules for related contracts, grants, and leases are suspended until January 2026.
- Mayoral Authority: The Mayor can approve certain contracts and leases if the Board of Supervisors does not act within 45 days.
- Lease Approvals: Board approval is not required for leases related to these initiatives until January 2026.
- Funding & Donations: The City can accept up to $10 million in private donations and grants for these efforts. The Mayor’s Office can also solicit donations for six months, overriding existing restrictions.
- Budget Flexibility: The Controller can reallocate surplus funds for these initiatives.
- Sunset Date: A prior waiver for homelessness-related contracts will expire in May 2029.

ordinance: Lodi — Municipal code 8.04
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Municipal code 8.04 - camping ordinance introduced post-Grants Pass. I don't think it's passed yet. Current camping ordinance is about needing a permit to erect a tent, the new proposal is standard, but not super expansive (includes mattresses, but not sleeping bags/pillows).

ordinance: San Joaquin County — Chapter 7
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: San Joaquin Board of Supervisors, on 10/24/2024, adopted a county ordinance on camping, post-Grants Pass. Title 6, Division 3, Chapter 7. The ordinance is expansive (includes all bedding) and includes fines of up to $1,000 and up to six months incarceration. Also extends to living in vehicles. ordinance: Stockton — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Unanimous City Council vote for a camping ordinance that prohibits unhoused people from camping on public property. Known as the "Critical Infrastructure Ordinance," it imposes daily penalties between $250-$25,000. Includes shelter and vehicles and "paraphernalia," including sleeping bags.

ordinance: Tracy — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Notes from a meeting on Grants Pass noted that Tracy was supportive of the Grants Pass decision and was already "ahead of the curve" on enforcement.

ordinance: San Mateo — 5.80.0309
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
(1)Ordinance 5.80.0309  effected on Mar, 2024 rules that no person shall establish, construct, maintain, operate or conduct any camp, as herein defined, without first complying with the provisions of this chapter and obtaining a License as herein provided.
(2) "Hopeful Horizons" passed in Jan., 2024:
3.109.030 Encampment Prohibitions: It is illegal and considered a public nuisance to set up or maintain a homeless encampment on public property in unincorporated areas if a shelter is available.This rule does not apply if the individual has not been offered shelter or lacks the means to obtain it.
Two warnings must be issued before a violation is enforced. Failure to leave within 24 hours of the second warning leads to a misdemeanor. Law enforcement can remove personal belongings if necessary. Alternative programs may be available instead of criminal penalties.

ordinance: Atherton — Municipal Code 12.16.010-
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
Municipal Code 12.16.010-Camping or building fires or Picnic on public street prohibited.
Atherton’s ordinance banning overnight camping in vehicles was finalized on Nov. 20 by a unanimous vote of the City Council.
However, there is a proposed ordinance would permit people experiencing homelessness to be charged with a misdemeanor after receiving two written warnings and declining two offers of shelter. The ordinance defines an encampment as any "tent, makeshift structure or collection of belongings" located in an area not meant for habitation and with no set plans to move.

ordinance: Belmont — Sec. 16-29
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Belmont City Code Sec. 16-29. limits on special activities like overnight camping except at locations specifically designated for such activities.

ordinance: Brisbane — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
12, 2024In Brisbane from Nov, it is illegal to camp overnight in a vehicle on a road or road related area (e.g. footpath, dividing strip or public cycle paths) unless it is in a designated signed area or tourist accommodation area.
Council may issue you with a fine if you camp in your vehicle overnight on a road or park in Brisbane.
Sleeping in a vehicle is not prohibited when:
it is required for fatigue management
it is required for personal safety
otherwise in emergent circumstances.

ordinance: Broadmoor — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: No local ordinance found. Refer to the county ordinance.

ordinance: Burlingame — § 13.32.190(
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
In Code of Ordinance§ 13.32.190(Ord. 1259 § 2, (1983)), no person shall use any trailer, semitrailer, camper, camp trailer, house car, trailer coach, mobile home or any other vehicle for the purpose of sleeping or living quarters whether on public or private property.
In Burlingame State Park, visitors must leave by 10 PM.
Only one family is allowed per campsite.
Non-family groups are limited to six people per campsite.
Cabins are limited to six people, one tent, and two vehicles.
Campsites are allowed two vehicles maximum.
The number of tents allowed on a site varies by campground.

ordinance: Colma — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text: Colma is planing a project named HOPE (Housing Our People Effectively),a ten-year action plan initiated by San Mateo County that brings together the business, nonprofit, and public sector communities to address the challenging issue of homelessness.The plan will increasing the supply of permanent affordable and supportive housing for people who are homeless and developing strategies to help them move into housing as rapidly as possible; and prevent individuals and families from becoming homeless in the first place by assisting them to maintain their housing

ordinance: Daly City — §10.76.020
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
§10.76.020 It is prohibited to use or occupy any vehicle, camper, mobile home, RV, or similar conveyance for human habitation on public property (such as streets, alleys, parks, beaches, or squares) between 10:00 p.m. and 6:00 a.m.
Exception: Only allowed in areas officially designated by the city for such purposes.Violating this ordinance is considered an infraction and subject to fines:
First violation: Up to $50 fine.
Second violation (within one year): Up to $100 fine.
Additional violations (within one year): Up to $250 fine.

ordinance: Foster City — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
No local ordinance found. Refer to the county ordinance.
Foster City has resources for people experiencing homelessness, including housing and support services.

ordinance: Redwood City — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
The ordinance, passed on Jan. 2024, makes refusing to leave an encampment a misdemeanor after two warnings, but it cannot be enforced unless a shelter bed is available and has been declined, which is in line with the law which states that an unhoused person cannot be criminally charged for taking up public space if no shelter is made available to them
The Police Department determines if an encampment meets a certain threshold for posing a risk to public safety, public health, or environmental concerns. If a site meets the threshold, then the Police Department coordinates with Public Works to set the date for the encampment clean up, typically two weeks in advance. The Police Department then posts written “seven-day notices to vacate” throughout encampment locations advising of the cleanup date, the consequences of failure to vacate (including potential citation or arrest), and offering access to services. On the day of the clean-up, any remaining residents are given a final opportunity to vacate and collect their belongings before the clean-up begins and any enforcement action is taken. Personal property is stored for 90 days and the City allows owners to retrieve property during that time period.

The government is investing heavily to implement strategies in partnership with property owners, including the State, and service providers, including the County and a host of non-profit agencies.

ordinance: San Bruno — § 24-2
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
San Bruno Ordinance has a rule about trail camp:  no person shall own, operate or manage, or permit to be owned, operated or managed, upon any premises or property under his or her control any trailer camp in the city, without having first applied for and received permission for such operation or maintenance from the city council. No such trailer camp shall be maintained in any residential portion of the city, nor within the portion of the commercial district of the city embraced within the confines of fire zone 2 of the city.(Prior code § 24-2; Ord. 1878 § 4, 2020)
Encampments cannot be immediately removed—multiple outreach attempts and housing offers must be made first.
Individuals who claim interest in housing but delay the process create legal challenges for enforcement.
Strict documentation and storage of personal belongings are required when an encampment is cleared.
Santa Barbara Police Department must prove all support options were exhausted before removal is legally justified.

ordinance: South San Francisco — § 3, 1937)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
South San Francisco Ordinance has a rule about trailor camp: It is unlawful for any person, firm or corporation to erect, construct, establish, alter, operate, maintain or offer for public use within the confines of the city, as hereinafter set forth, any campground without first applying for and receiving from the city council a permit so to do, in the manner hereinafter provided, and without complying with regulations hereinafter set forth, and any rules and regulations which may be formulated by the health officer from time to time and with the laws of the state.
(Ord. 193 § 3, 1937)

ordinance: Modesto — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Modesto’s Camping and Storage Ordinance (Article 7 effective 7-12-12)：
Camping and storage of personal items on public or private property are prohibited unless explicitly allowed.
Temporary camping is permitted on private property for up to 5 nights with consent.
Special event camping permits can be issued at the City Manager’s discretion.
Violations are misdemeanors and can result in legal or administrative actions，including fines, penalties, and enforcement mechanisms
The City Manager has authority to regulate, approve, or revoke permits.

ordinance: Turlock — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
(Effective 11/24)Individuals cannot sit, lie, or sleep in public spaces, particularly in designated sensitive-use areas.
The City has the authority to enforce removal, issue citations, and impose misdemeanor penalties including  up to one (1) year in jail and/or a $1,000 fine.
Personal property cannot be left in public places and will be removed after 24 hours of notice.

ordinance: Ceres — Municipal Code §18.28.120
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Ceres Municipal Code §18.28.120(1-25-2021):Camping is prohibited in most public and private areas within residential, commercial, and industrial zones.
Limited exceptions allow camping in designated campgrounds, permitted events, and certain residential RV situations.
Violators receive an 8-hour warning before enforcement.
Multiple violations lead to misdemeanor charges  and punishable by a fine of not more than $1,000.00, or by imprisonment in the County jail for a period not exceeding six months, or by both such fine and imprisonment.
Citations may be dismissed if no shelter is available and the person had no other reasonable alternatives.
The Critical Infrastructure Protection Ordinance will declare it a public nuisance for any person to camp, occupy camp facilities, use camp paraphernalia, or store personal property within 25 feet of critical infrastructure, within 25 feet of a vehicular or pedestrian entrance or exit of critical infrastructure, and in portions of right-of-way that are required to clear to first-responders.
The second ordinance calls for the city to provide written notice advising occupants they have 72 hours to clear out before the city commences a clean-up operation – unless conditions are so severe that immediate action is required.

ordinance: Riverbank — §130.02)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
The loitering ordinance (§130.02) could potentially apply to homeless individuals camping in public spaces,If a homeless individual is camping or staying in a public space in a manner that raises safety concerns (e.g., blocking a sidewalk, refusing to identify themselves, or attempting to conceal themselves), they could be subject to this provision.
However, if they provide a valid explanation (e.g., lack of shelter), this could prevent conviction.

ordinance: Oakdale — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Article XII: Camping is prohibited in public spaces and private property without consent, except for limited exemptions.
Special event camping requires a permit, subject to health and safety review.
Illegal camping and personal property storage are misdemeanors and can be enforced as public nuisances.

ordinance: Newman — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
(Passed December 10, 2024.)Camping is prohibited on public and private property, except in limited situations.
Storage of camping-related items is also banned.
Providing aid to unlawful campers is restricted.
Temporary camping permits are available for special events.
Violations are misdemeanors but may be dismissed if no alternative shelter was available.

ordinance: Waterford — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
(2024-12-05)Camping and setting up shelters on public land are illegal.
Sleeping outdoors is prohibited at night, unless no indoor shelter is available.
Specific areas mentioned include city parks, riverbanks, and under bridges.
This ordinance is likely influenced by legal rulings protecting homeless individuals from penalties when shelters are full.

ordinance: Sebastopol — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Ordinance passed that is enforced starting January 2025, where RV parking is prohibited at all times on residential streets in Sebastopol and is allowed in commercial or industrial zones only between 10 pm and 7 am. Former mayor Diana Rich, who voted for the RV ordinance in March 2022, called it a “compassionate compromise” that allows people to sleep in Sebastopol in their RVs, but not set up permanent encampments.

ordinance: Santa Rosa — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
The county in 2023  enacted a ban on camping on public property from 7 a.m. to 9 p.m. daily, among other prohibitions. Santa Rosa’s ordinance, also passed in 2023, prohibits people from setting up tents or storing belongings in city parks or on the sidewalk — among other public areas — if it impedes traffic.
Both the city and county between 2019 and 2023 operated under a federal injunction limiting their ability to clear illegal encampments on public property. Challenged in federal court, injunction, but Grants knocked down main argument.

ordinance: Visalia — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: The changes to sections 12.32 and 12.35 of Visalia’s municipal code allow police to resume detaining people living in city parks or to force them to leave. The alterations also renew the city’s authority to remove highly-visible encampments established by unhoused citizens on public property.

ordinance: Tulare — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: The existing camping ordinance in Tulare, which prevents camping in particular parks in the city, “is certainly legal at this point,” said Zamora at the city council meeting, adding that the city does not need to take any action as a result of the ruling. “We didn't have on our books a lot of ordinances,” said Tulare County Supervisor Dennis Townsend about homelessness, adding that the county was “pretty much just going by state law as far as removing someone” from a homeless encampment.

ordinance: Porterville — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: The City of Porterville’s Unsafe Camping Ordinance, approved in April 2024, established new regulations prohibiting camping around critical infrastructure and codifying how, when and where the City may abate or conduct enforcement related to violations of the regulations. Enforcement of the ordinance began May 16, 2024.

ordinance: Vallejo — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: $175,000 set aside this month for “unhoused strategic plan,” Murray said the city is putting together a selection panel this month to meet and recommend a vendor who will handle drafting the plan, after which the city council can approve the project scope and contractor.

ordinance: Vacaville — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
It is unlawful and a public nuisance for any person or group of persons to camp, occupy camping facilities, or use camping paraphernalia in the following areas:
A. Any public property, improved or unimproved, including, but not limited to, public streets and sidewalks, parks, open space, and other property. Same for storage of property in public area.

ordinance: Fairfield — (no code section cited in source)
name: Policy change
text: Anti-camping ordinances penalty voted just this week to change from infraction to misdo.

ordinance: Carpinteria — Chapter 8.20
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapter 8.20 of the Carpinteria Municipal Code covers Sleeping and Camping. Although it does not specifiy what properties are designated for overnight camping, the municipal code does allow for overnight camping in those places. Otherwise, camping is not permitted and a violation is a misdemeanor or an infraction. Violators receive a warning prior to citation and are referred to services for persons experiencing homelessness, if appropriate.

ordinance: Santa Maria — Section 6-1.02
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Section 6-1.02 Camping and storage of personal property in certain areas prohibited. This section largely bans overnight camping, whether inside or outside vehicles. Violation of this section of the code is a misdemeanor. The section does not prohibit camping in areas designated for such purposes for the City Council, but it does not specify in the code what those sections are.

ordinance: Goleta — Municipal Code §10.04.040
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Municipal Code §10.04.040 Sleeping, Camping, Use of a Recreational Vehicle for Occupancy on Any Public Street, Highway, Right-of-War or Any Public Property Within the City—Prohibited. Does not allow any overnight parking or camping in a trailer.

ordinance: Solvang — Chapter 6
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapter 6, Camping, §5-6-3 Camping on city property: Solvang still has a carveout in their municpal code for "people who do not have any other permanent residence or domicile and/or are involuntarily homeless are not prohibited from camping on public property or rights-of-way, provided camping occurs in compliance with certain rules and regulations outlined in the code.

ordinance: Lompoc — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Past but very recent. Ordinance was passed on March 4, 2025. City Council banned personal belongins or camping on public property. New ordinances will prohibit camping on any public or private property with the exceptions of campgrounds and the permission of the private property owner; prohibit storing personal property in a way that obstructs doorways, sidewalsk, or other areas public property; prohibit a person from using their body to do the same; and prohibit camping, storing personal property, or taking up more space than one seated person in bust stop shelters or on bust stop benches. Council passed these new ordinance unanimously, 5-0 and cited the Grants Pass.

ordinance: Solvang — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: In October, 2023, Solvang Mayor Mark Infanti signed a new ordinance to allow RVs to be parked in residential neighborhoods and along city streets with restrictions. It had been previously been prohibited. Under the new ordinance, temprorary parking is permitted for 24 hours a a time on city-owned public property, excluding areas within Solvang's Tourism Commercial District. Overnight parking is still prohibited downtown, near schools, or in areas that impede traffic. Once a vehicle has been parked in the same place for more than 24 hours, it will be asked to relocate.

ordinance: Santa Barbara — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: The Clean Community Division of the City of Santa Barbara facillitates a program called "Clean SB" dedicated to keeping the City clean through programs focused on litter clean up, illegal dumping, abandonded shopping carts, and encampments. The response team for this program consists of representaties from City Net (a contractor) and represenatives from throughout the City. The team works to connect unsheltered individuals with services, clean up encampments, and take enforcement action. The team typically provides 72 hours advance notice of clean ups. The city reports 1,191 encampment cleanups since 2019. Notably absent is the amount of people housed from these encampments.

ordinance: Santa Barbara — Chapter 9.98
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapter 9.98: Pedestrians Blocking Public Sidewalks. No person shall stand, or sit, or congregate in or upon any street, sidewalk or crosswalk in the City with the intent to hinder or obstruct the free passage of pedestrians thereon, or to annoy or molest such pedestrians, or to block the entrance to a building, and refuse to disperse after having been ordered to do so by the police when the police reasonably believe an immediate threat to public safety is present. Penalty is a misdemenaor, and punishment of a fine not exceeding $500, imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months, or both.

ordinance: Santa Barbara — (no code section cited in source)
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapet 9.97: Sitting or Lying on Sidewalks and Paseos Along Certain Downtown Portions of State Street. Deisgnates specific parts in downtown Santa Barbara where it is prohibited to sit or lie down on a sidewalk or public paseo, or upon a blanket, chair, stool, or any other object between 7:00am and 2:00am. Unclear from the chapter what the punishment is but it does state that no one can be prosecuted for a violation of this chapter unless the person engaged in the prohibited conudct after having been notified by a law enforcement officer or other City-designated volunteer or employee.

ordinance: Santa Barbara — Chapter 9.50
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text: Chapter 9.50: Prohibition of Abusive Panhandling. This ordinance restricts abusive panhandling across the city of Snata Barbara. Abusive panhandling is defined as blocking or impeding someone's movement, following someone, threateneing them, touching the person, among other things. Furthermore, the ordinance restricts active panhandling (a verbal request) in many locations like bus stops, in a vehicle, in a City parking lot, within 25 feet of a restaurant, among other things. Any person who violates the ordinance shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, fined at most $1,000, or imprisoned for no more than 6 months, or both.

ordinance: Santa Barbara — (no code section cited in source)
name: Policy change
text: **Santa Barbara County, Not City** In January of this year, in the wake of the Grants Pass decision, the Santa Barbara County Supervisors adopted some changes, though not radical shifts, to their approach to dealing with encapments. Newly approved procedures stiipulated that encapments may only be cleared if alternative shelter is avaliable, with exceptions for publcic safety. These exceptions include encapments in waterways or areas where the use of flammables near dry brush heightens the risk of fire. Policy changes also allow for less notice before encampments are cleared and a shorter duration for storing uncollected belgomings.

ordinance: Salinas — (no code section cited in source)
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
Article XIIII of Salinas Municipal code (passed October 10, 2017) prohibits storing unattended personal property on city land, limits attended property to a volume fitting within a 90-gallon container, and bans storing items that obstruct pathways, entrances, or violate ADA requirements. Article XIV (passed October 10, 2017) prohibits camping in public areas.
The city can impound personal property with notice, or immediately remove items that pose health or safety threats or block essential areas. Notices must detail the removal process, property descriptions, and how property can be reclaimed within 90 days before disposal.
Additionally, the ordinance forbids attaching personal belongings to public or private property in a way that obstructs public movement, with immediate removal allowed.
The Salinas Homeless Union is one of the organizations based in Salinas advocates not only for visibly unhoused people but also for poor and vulnerable individuals living precariously according to the news of January 20, 2025.

ordinance: Seaside — Chapter 9.20
name: Anti-camping/enforcement provision
text:
Chapter 9.20 of Seaside Municipal Code current through Ordinance 2010, passed October 7, 2021 prohibits people from using vehicles, campers, or trailers for sleeping, eating, or resting on public property in the city between 10:00 PM and 6:00 AM unless explicitly allowed by posted signs. Additionally, camping, sleeping, or erecting tents or any temporary sleeping facilities on city-owned public property is banned entirely. Violations are punishable as infractions.
The city has a homeless commission, and its meeting on March 5, 2025 reviewed its Annual Work Plan for the fiscal year of 2024-2025, which was adopted April 3, 2024. The commission will focus on expanding housing and emergency support, strengthening community partnerships, and enhancing services for the unhoused. Key initiatives include: identifying new transitional housing sites, advocating for rental assistance, improving safe parking programs, collaborating with food and youth support organizations, and exploring mobile hygiene services. The commission will also seek funding opportunities, host community forums, and push for policy changes to prevent and address homelessness.

ordinance: Monterey — Chapter 22
name: Proposed anti-camping provision
text:
The "Homeless Response" page of City of Monterey accessed on February 23, 2025 listed related City Codes, which addresses various regulations concerning motor vehicles, public conduct, parks, streets, and sidewalks to maintain community safety and order.
